title: The blue-collar jobs revival: The skills the world needs now | FT Working It

transcript.text: Everything [music] from construction to
skilled labor, electricians, plumbers,
mechanicals, the whole thing. We're
going to need a lot of [music] skilled
labor to support all the AI factories we
have to build.
>> Demand for those who can work alongside
machines in factories and frontline jobs
is high. But what does good work look
like in the age of AI?
I'm Isabelle Barrett. I lead the FT's
working in brand, speaking, presenting
and writing about management, leadership
and workplaces. In this series, [music]
I'll explore some of the most pressing
issues around the future of work and
talk to senior leaders about how they
are making work better.
>> There's a magic to human relationship
and human connection
>> for everyone.
AI and tech transformation are upending
the traditional hierarchies and status
of the work world. A lot of attention
has been [music] given to AI's potential
effects on white collar clerical and
professional [music] jobs. But focus is
now shifting to frontline deskless
workers. I think the [music] real
possibility here is that there'll be
some what we now call white collar jobs
which are eradicated or commoditized by
AI and other technologies and there will
be some hands-on jobs be it caring which
we might now classify as a blue collar
job or plumbing electrician or or trade
and apprentice type jobs that are
actually given more credit. First of all
these data centers need building. So
we're going to need a lot of people to
do that and that of course is an
immediate positive hit to an economy
that is building data centers.
>> In recent decades, the push to expand
higher education has led to a greater
focus on graduate level jobs and that's
created a shortage of people going into
skilled blue collar work. But with the
[music] massive investments being made
in infrastructure, particularly for the
digital future, skilled trades people
are in huge demand. Over the next 5
years, more than 2,000 new data centers
are set to [music] be built globally,
and that's likely to require more than
new technicians and engineers.
I visited Equinix, Paris, one of
Europe's largest data centers, for a
rare tour inside this secure site.
>> Often when we talk about the internet,
when we talk about the cloud, Wow.
>> we believe it's all virtual.
>> Yeah. Yes.
>> Okay. But it's everything but virtual.
It's made of infrastructure. It's made
of building. There are people who
organize that.
>> Equinex has 273 data centers globally
where they house and maintain data
storage devices, servers, and networking
cables needed to deliver information
around the world. So the popular idea of
a data center is like a vast warehouse
with very few people needed to run it.
What's the reality? Just imagine that a
data center is a kind of combination
between an airport hub, a factory or a
logistic platform. So it's a very busy
environment with a lot of flows of
people of goods of of live equipment.
Maybe what is important to understand is
that internet in general became a
utility. Hence the the number of layers
of infrastructure that we put in a data
center to make sure that in case of an
event it does not develop to an outage.
This is what our customers and the
consumers are looking for.
>> Okay.
>> So thinking about the people who work
here, what are the kind of jobs that are
on offer here and how is that developing
as the facilities get bigger? So the
competencies start with the design
people and the construction people. So
we need people specialized in
electrical, mechanical, um,
architectural to design it. Then we need
people to build it and then it will last
for the next 40 years. Then here we have
people who will maintain the facility.
They will perform preventive
maintenance, corrective maintenance and
even what we call predictive
maintenance. And we do that seven days a
week, 24 hours a day.
>> So traditional skills like plumber,
electrician, carpenter, all of those
things are needed too.
>> Absolutely. Those are essential
competencies that will continue to be
needed in the data center industry. The
thing is that we'll have to to adapt to
the uh how we'll combine those
competencies and to adapt to the pace at
which the technologies that are required
to deliver our customers will will
appear.
>> So we're just at the beginning of this
kind of extraordinary tech
transformation. How big could the data
center industry get, do you think?
>> Simple answer. Uh and just to give you a
flavor, within the next five years, we
intend to bring
additional capacity, the additional
capacity equivalent to what we did in
the last 27 years.
>> To keep up with the rapidly evolving
demand for data center skills, Equinex
[music] runs a specialized
apprenticeship scheme.
Right now we're looking at real customer
fibers and behind them there are
customers and their services are right
now working because of these
connections. Each and every outlet you
have behind it cables that go to a
certain server. So the server represents
a client. So let's say you plug it in
the wrong one, it's going to go to the
wrong server and you're going to create
a crossconnect between two clients who
do not want to share information
together. when you talk to other people
about what you do, is it the kind of
thing that people are thinking about?
>> Um, nowadays people are really thinking
about uh these types of jobs, especially
with the fact that we're moving towards
a future that is AI generated and the AI
is housed in a data center. So we can
project in the future and we can see
that AI is going to be needed. So data
center engineers, technicians, managers,
everyone that can work in a data center
is going to be needed.
>> The tech transformation may create a
faster, more profound upheaval for
deskless [music]
workers than for the laptop classes. The
ADCO group, whose clients are largely in
labor industries, [music]
conducted a survey of over 37,000
workers on their expectations for the
future of work. I met with the group's
CEO, Denim Ashwell, to hear more about
the survey and the trends he's seeing.
>> The first thing that that they tell us
is they want to understand first of all
the purpose of what they do, what's the
meaning of the things that they do, how
useful they are. That's one thing. Um
the second thing is um understand also
what's going to happen to me with this
AI revolution coming in. 30% of skills
uh become obsolescent uh over four year
over four years uh and people need to be
fundamentally ready to constantly learn.
>> Do you think that skilled trades are
always going to be in demand because
they certainly are at the moment.
>> They certainly are and we believe that
uh again robots won't replace everyone.
They will replace part of jobs but not
the whole role. And uh yeah, we we
believe that these kind of uh uh
expertise that have been um overlooked
sometimes or that have not been valued
as much as uh as uh as they should over
time uh will will remain.
>> Can you see a future where the pay
charts flip and what we would
traditionally call professional or white
collar work is eroded and you know
people will want their kids to go into
the trades. We see that coming in many
of the European countries. For example,
if you look at how much an electrician
uh can make versus someone that has a
professional clerical job which can be
jeopardized by AI uh the electrician can
make more money. So we we see this uh
inversion starting difficult to uh
really evaluate how far this is going to
go. But these type of skills have been
undervalued
uh the in many of the European countries
except for example Switzerland and
Germany where vocational training has
been mainstream uh for for many many
years.
>> Can you speculate how many jobs will go?
You know if you've got a big factory
with 500 people in it at the moment in
10 years time how many of those human
jobs are still going to be there? So
first of all it's very difficult to
model this uh on a mac on a micro
perspective
uh yes as soon as you automate you have
less uh you have less workers by essence
you need different type of skills you
still have those deskless or blue color
workers or new color workers as we call
them because they are they are mix of uh
of traditional type of roles plus the
technical savviness that they need to
have on the micro element you will see
probably less jobs. But on a macro
uh perspective,
this creates other jobs elsewhere. Even
though you have less jobs in the
warehouse, you have other jobs that are
being created, for example, to uh to
produce these um to produce this uh
these machines or to maintain these
machines uh or to do troubleshooting.
what we've seen and if history can be of
any of any guide every technology
revolution has over time created as much
if not more jobs than it has replaced.
>> Deni highlighted that to fulfill the
quot of skilled trades people needed
older people in manual jobs will have to
stay in the workforce for longer. AI and
robots can help with the more physical
work and companies need to entice more
young people to join. I think we've
really seen a huge shift in the opinion
of young people on bluecollar work. As
the cost of college has gone up, it's
become even more difficult to justify
taking on hundreds of thousands of
dollars of debt if that's what you have
to do to get a degree. We are now in a
place where you have people searching on
TikTok every day saying, "What is it
like to be an electrician or to become a
mechanic or an air conditioning repair
person?" And and that is something that
I think 5 years ago we never ever would
have predicted. I
>> first heard about fiber.
[music]
>> For many young people, the corporate
contract is broken. It's incredibly hard
to get a job. And once there, you may be
[music] asked to work crazy hours or
fired at any time. No wonder there's
something appealing about a skilled
trade. [music] In a recent international
labor organization assessment, the
sector ranked [music] among the least
vulnerable to AI was craft work. I
visited York Minster and its center
[music] of excellence for heritage
crafts where the next generation of
stonemasons are being trained. [music]
>> So do you think that traditional skills
like stonemasonry are getting more
popular? We hear that Jenzie are getting
much more interested in working with
their hands, you know, work with
purpose. the corporate life is not so
appealing. Was that part of your
thinking or can you see that playing out
among your friends?
>> If you do the whole project beginning to
end, you feel responsibility for it um
and you feel like you've made something
at the end of it. And from what other
people have said, um who are working in,
you know, more corporate jobs, um you
don't always get that opportunity to
feel like you're doing something that's
important. You know, you might get more
caught up with meetings and things that
don't necessarily have an end.
>> Don't know what you're talking about.
So, so we're seeing a lot of what we
might call white collar jobs
disappearing. People are worried about
the future of their career.
>> How do you see your career? Do you see
it as something with stability?
>> I think that the idea of what a
stonemason does will always be a
necessary career. I don't think it's
going to disappear. I think that's the
case for a lot of trades. The joy of
working in this kind of setting now is
that we get to do something with our
hands that's very productive that feels
very satisfying and very creative and it
encourages a lot of job- based social
interactions that I think not all
careers have that just the pure
satisfaction of doing something that
feels like you're doing something
productive [music] with a legacy with a
proper purpose every day.
>> [music]
>> Apprentices like Freya are keeping the
centuries old skill alive and helping to
maintain heritage sites like [music]
Yorkminster.
Alex Mallian is director [music] of
works and precinct there.
>> So when we think about the jobs of the
future and we've come here to a very
ancient cathedral where you're training
the next generation. What are the jobs
that you're training them [music] for?
uh stonemasonry um but also uh we've got
electricians uh plumbers, lead workers,
joiners. We have an amazing uh gardening
team who are responsible for all of the
gardens and the and the estate. So the
the full range of crafts people um
needed to look after a complex estate
such as this.
>> So it looks like AI is going to take
quite a lot of jobs in the what we might
call the white collar world.
>> What's the future looking like for the
jobs in your sector? Well, we've used uh
technology as a tool to support our
work. So, uh it's certainly not
replacing uh jobs. Uh it's there to
support uh and to broaden skills. We are
using photoggramometry, uh 3D modeling,
scanning. We are looking potentially at
how technology can help us look at
identifying issues with the stone. So,
there are opportunities to use it to our
advantage. And interestingly, of course,
the medieval masons who built this
wonderful building were always pushing
the boundaries of technology at the time
and engineering and architecture. So,
all we're doing is continuing that
tradition.
I think craft jobs have a a natural
appeal in a world where we are
screenbased and screen obsessed. We're
not doing very much with our hands when
we are looking at our phones or tablets
or computer screens. we are not doing
very much with our heads when we are
doing that because we are scrolling
addictive social media and so I think
for a younger generation that's been
very much brought up on screen the idea
of being able to switch off literally
the devices and concentrate you know as
John Ruskin said the sort of hand heart
and head on a particular task one that
yields a thing of beauty is very very
attractive either as a hobby or indeed
as a profession.
>> The snag is there aren't ever going to
be very many craft workers who are able
to professionally pursue their crafts.
I think a very important caveat to all
this excitement about the opportunities
in the bluecollar sectors is that as
hiring has really pulled back over the
past year, it has been those sectors
that have cut the job postings the most.
So while it's really difficult and
frustrating to get a job in corporate
America right now, statistically they
are doing most of the hiring. Now a lot
of that hiring is for more senior
[music] and experienced people. When you
think about the industries that tend
[music] to slow down first when the
economy deteriorates, it is
transportation, it is warehousing, it is
logistics, it is construction. [music]
Interest rates are higher right now than
they've been in a long time. So fewer
houses are being built. That means you
need fewer people working construction,
working in those skilled trades.
Consumer spending [music] is looking a
little shaky, which means potentially
fewer jobs at your local Amazon
warehouse. I [music] think there is a
meaningful trend here, particularly in
how young people are speaking about
their career opportunities, but I want
to caution [music] people from thinking
that if you go to trade school, you are
guaranteed a six-f figureure job because
that is just not the reality in this job
market.
[music] There's a danger that humans
will end up machine minding, watching
the robots doing the jobs that used to
be ours. But in the meantime, it's
likely that many more people will seek
jobs with purpose as the tech
transformation gathers [music] speed.
